We have three methods in self._blocking_methods. The first time through the loop, fut.done() will be False, but if for some reason you sent more than one of them in *expected_methods, the future would be set twice raising an InvalidStateError.

If none of the _blocking_methods are included, the loop proceeds and sets an exception to kill the waiting task.

I think it it's ok to return a transaction dict without a `nonce`.  In fact, I'm inclined to have `nonce` inclusion be opt-in, here's my reasoning.

* If I'm preparing a *generic* transaction that I want to send to many different contracts, I don't want `nonce` included.
* If I'm preparing a transaction to be sent once, not including nonce won't hurt because the `web3.eth.sendTransaction` API (and underlying `eth_sendTransaction` JSON-RPC) automatically add the proper nonce.

I think that fully excluding `nonce` from the return dict unless it's included in the `transaction` parameter would be just as easy to work with and would make using this API with different use cases easier.

Changing the behaviour of the jobtree etc to use a repr instead of a str changes the display behaviour of the job.
Before the ```repr``` function was used jobtree and subjobs would display correctly in the summary of a job. Now they display a really long line with formatting and ```\n``` characters and such. Using a string method to parse this gets back to the original intended behaviour.
Interactive matches the interactive mode of being on the IPython interactive prompt. We expect subtly different behaviour of some functions when we're using this for displaying the output. (Sure we could do some introspection and change the behaviour dynamically right at the repr/string level but this becomes overkill imo)
The default behaviour hasn't changed unless the VPrinter is called with pretty_repr for storing files to disk, in which case it's a simple matter to expand the API there to accept 1 additional boolean argument

Support Linux watchdog devices to reboot the master if Patroni is not able to operate in a timely manner to uphold
    single master guarantee. This protects against Patroni process getting terminated by bugs or external causes.
    (e.g. kernel OOM killer) It also protects against situations where Patroni is not able to disable the master
    before leader key expires. (e.g. when the database is on an unresponsive networked disk)

    When watchdog mechanism is enabled Patroni will set it up so that watchdog will terminate the system before any
    other system becomes eligible for leader. Watchdog enables Patroni to give almost as good guarantees as traditional
    clustering systems provide with a much more complicated STONITH mechanism.

    It is possible to configure the watchdog device (`/dev/watchdog` by default) and the mode (required, automatic, off) 
    from the watchdog section of the Patroni configuration. By default Patroni will try to use the watchdog device
    if the permissions allow, but will start without if the device is missing or not accessible. You can get more
    information from the :ref:`watchdog documentation<watchdog>`.

I looked at the other methods defined by Python list objects and several are missing and will fall back to the list implementation. For example, the lack of `__mul__()` means that `UnspacedList(["foo", " ", "bar"])*3` is just the list `["foo", "bar", "foo", "bar", "foo", "bar"]` without a `.spaced` attribute.

In some cases, this is desirable; for example, for `__getitem__`, the behavior of the underlying list is exactly correct. It is arguably also correct for `__eq__` and other comparisons. In other cases, it may result in some confusion.

I see these list methods as not specifically implemented here: `__class__`, `__contains__`, `__delattr__`, `__delslice__`, `__doc__`, `__eq__`, `__format__`, `__ge__`, `__getattribute__`, `__getitem__`, `__getslice__`, `__gt__`, `__hash__`, `__iadd__`, `__imul__`, `__init__`, `__iter__`, `__le__`, `__len__`, `__lt__`, `__mul__`, `__ne__`, `__new__`, `__reduce__`, `__reduce_ex__`, `__repr__`, `__reversed__`, `__rmul__`, `__setattr__`, `__sizeof__`, `__str__`, `__subclasshook__`, `count`, `index`, `pop`, `remove`, `reverse`, `sort`.

Many of these are arguably right to use the underlying list implementation (for example, things like `index`, `count`, and `__len__` are credible). I am still concerned about at least `pop`, `remove`, `reverse`, and `sort`, which all modify the list. I tested trying to `reverse` an `UnspacedList`, and it caused the `.spaced` version to become severely desynchronized with the user-visible content.

I think the right answer is probably "anything that generates a new list with the entire content of the old list, or that modifies the existing object, should either be reimplemented or raise a `NotImplementedError`". So I have 4 specific examples of things I think need one or the other treatment. I think this is prudent even if the existing code doesn't call any of those methods, to avoid hard-to-debug cases if the parser is modified in the future.

We did break the promise made with semantic versioning due to the API behavior change in 1.2.0 and the backward incompatible change between 1.2.0 and 1.2.1 patch release. As a community we are apologizing for the inconvenience caused and will continue to strive to uphold semantic versioning.

(A quick overview of task states. This isn't necessarily how things *should be* but it is how they *are* :) )

Indeed -- all tasks used to start with `State.NONE`. @bolkedebruin's latest PR introduces `State.SCHEDULED` when the TI has been created by the scheduler but not yet run. When the executor loads a TI, it eventually calls TI.run() which does one of two things:
1. If the TI can be run, sets `State.RUNNING` and calls the `execute()` method.
2. If the TI has a pool, sets `State.QUEUED` and returns
The scheduler has a `prioritize_queued` method which loads up all the queued tasks and tries to run them if there are slots available in their respective pools. That second run is the one that actually moves pooled tasks to `State.RUNNING`.
